channel.title: Posts on Justin's Musings
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Justin's Musings

item.title: Abuse Python for Fun(ction)s
item.description: I know that I shouldn’t have these “crazy” ideas, but sometimes they just jump into my mind. Recently, I was speaking to a coworker that is on a Linux learning journey, and he asked me about some of the bash scripting nuances. We were discussing some of the different operations available in bash such as @ and |. At that moment, I started thinking about how the | operator works in bash.

item.title: Securing a Raspberry Pi
item.description: Recently, I have been working on an automated water system. I am working on a proof of concept that would allow me to expand into more and more IoT projects for my house. In order to accomplish this, I am going to be running some services on a Raspberry Pi. Now, I know some of you are saying…. I want to add a firewall and change my network to separate these IoT devices from the rest of my home devices.

item.title: GitHub Actions With Go
item.description: Recently, I was working on a Go project (see GoCat and Remote Commands) in order to utilize Go’s wonderful cross platform support. However, when I think about rebuilding the binaries everytime that I make changes to the project, I just get tired. A little voice in my head says… Before we move forward on how I automated the build process, I will warn you that sometimes this voice can create work for you that is not meaningful.

item.title: GoCat and Remote Commands
item.description: This week at work, Daniel, a coworker, and I have been experimenting with replacing the usage of netcat for executing commands on a remote machine with a custom-built piece of software. Well, more specifically, Daniel ran into some issues with netcat, with the -e option, being absent from a machine that he was trying to exploit. (Don’t worry, it was for learning purposes and nothing malicious.) As a result, Daniel devised a simple replacement to a standalone netcat binary.

item.title: Microbenchmarking for Learning
item.description: I enjoy working on small puzzles to try to learn as well as keep my programming skills sharp. I find these puzzles in several different places such as Code Wars, Project Euler, or Exercism. Before going any further, do know there may be spoilers for your solution to the problem if you want to try Exercism: Scrabble Score. I like to try different things when solving these problems to explore whether the code is more readable, using a different data structure than may normally be used, or utilize different programming paradigm (ex.
